{{Short description="Thermal electromagnetic radiation emitted by a body in thermodynamic equilibrium"}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Black-body radiation
| image = Planck law log log.png
| caption = [[Planck's law]]: spectral radiance of a black body at various temperatures. The peak shifts to shorter wavelengths at higher temperatures ([[Wien's displacement law]]).
| discovered_by = [[Gustav Kirchhoff]] (1860, concept), [[Max Planck]] (1900, formula)
}}

'''Black-body radiation''' is the [[thermal radiation|thermal electromagnetic radiation]] within or surrounding a body in [[thermodynamic equilibrium]] with its environment, emitted by a [[black body]] (an idealized opaque, non-reflective body). It has a specific continuous [[electromagnetic spectrum|spectrum]] of wavelengths, inversely related to intensity, that depend only on the body's [[temperature]], which is assumed for the sake of calculations and theory to be uniform and constant.

The study of black-body radiation was pivotal in the development of [[quantum mechanics]]. The failure of [[classical physics]] to explain the observed spectrum — the [[ultraviolet catastrophe]] — led [[Max Planck]] to introduce the concept of [[quantization (physics)|energy quantization]] in 1900.

== Planck's law ==
{{Main|Planck's law}}
[[Max Planck]] derived the correct formula by postulating that electromagnetic energy is emitted and absorbed in discrete quanta <math>E = h\nu</math>:

The spectral radiance (power per unit area per unit solid angle per unit wavelength) is:
:<math>B_\lambda(\lambda, T) = \frac{2hc^2}{\lambda^5}\frac{1}{e^{hc/(\lambda k_B T)} - 1}</math>

In terms of frequency:
:<math>B_\nu(\nu, T) = \frac{2h\nu^3}{c^2}\frac{1}{e^{h\nu/(k_B T)} - 1}</math>

== Wein's displacement law ==
{{Main|Wien's displacement law}}
The wavelength at which the spectral radiance peaks is inversely proportional to temperature:
:<math>\lambda_{\text{max}} = \frac{b}{T}</math>
where <math>b \approx 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m·K}</math> is [[Wien's displacement constant]].

Examples:
* Sun (5778 K): <math>\lambda_{\text{max}} \approx 500\,\text{nm}</math> (green-yellow)
* Human body (310 K): <math>\lambda_{\text{max}} \approx 9.3\,\mu\text{m}</math> (infrared)
* Cosmic microwave background (2.725 K): <math>\lambda_{\text{max}} \approx 1.06\,\text{mm}</math> (microwave)

== Stefan–Boltzmann law ==
{{Main|Stefan–Boltzmann law}}
The total power radiated per unit area of a black body is:
:<math>j = \sigma T^4</math>
where <math>\sigma = 5.670\,374 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-4}</math> is the [[Stefan–Boltzmann constant]].

== Rayleigh–Jeans law and ultraviolet catastrophe ==
{{Main|Ultraviolet catastrophe}}
The classical [[Rayleigh–Jeans law]] predicted:
:<math>B_\lambda(\lambda, T) = \frac{2ck_B T}{\lambda^4}</math>

This diverges at short wavelengths (ultraviolet), predicting infinite radiated energy — the ''ultraviolet catastrophe''. Planck's quantization resolved this.

== Historical significance ==
* 1859: [[Gustav Kirchhoff]] defines the black body and posits the universality of its spectrum
* 1879: [[Josef Stefan]] discovers the <math>T^4</math> law; [[Ludwig Boltzmann]] derives it in 1884
* 1893: [[Wilhelm Wien]] derives the displacement law
* 1900: [[Max Planck]] presents his formula, introducing energy quantization — the birth of quantum mechanics
* 1905: [[Albert Einstein]] quantizes the radiation field (photons) to explain the [[photoelectric effect]]

== Applications ==
* [[Astronomy]] — determining stellar surface temperatures
* [[Cosmic microwave background]] — remnant of the Big Bang at 2.725 K
* [[Thermography]] — infrared cameras
* [[Incandescent light bulbs]]
* [[Climate science]] — Earth's energy balance
* [[Pyrometer]]s for temperature measurement

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Planck's law]]
* [[Ultraviolet catastrophe]]
* [[Wien's displacement law]]
* [[Stefan–Boltzmann law]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Black-body radiation| ]]
[[Category:Quantum mechanics]]
[[Category:Thermodynamics]]
